ledger_fix_plan.writer_summary: appendEventLedgerEvent (L21-38) is the SOLE writer. Uses raw fs/promises.appendFile (L35) with no fsync, no flock, no rotation. Path comes from getEventLedgerPath(projectRoot) → join(projectRoot, '.fabric/events.jsonl').
reader_files: packages/server/src/services/event-ledger.ts (readEventLedger L40-67 — production reader; parseEventLedgerLine L69-84 silently drops bad lines)
packages/server/src/services/read-ledger.ts (L179 readLedgerFromEventLedger reduces events into LedgerEntry projection)
packages/server/src/services/doctor.ts (L420 readEventLedger + L421-426 raw read for parseable check)

packages/server/src/api/events.ts (L192 readFileSize + L367-401 readEventLedgerAppendedEvents — incremental SSE tail reader; ALREADY tail-tolerant with eventLedgerRemainder; L432-452 parseEventLedgerAppendedEvent silently swallows errors)

all_callsites.note: appendFile (raw, no fsync) — the ONLY append site
JSON.parse + zod safeParse, returns null on failure
ensureEventLedger via writeFile flag:'a' for empty-create
fs.open + read range — ALREADY tail-tolerant (state.eventLedgerRemainder buffers trailing partial line)
writeFileSync(eventsPath, '') — creates empty file at scaffold time, single-shot

ledger_fix_plan.library_rationale: Recommendation: use `atomically.appendFile` for the append path. Reasoning: (1) `atomically` is TS-native (existing dep ecosystem aligned with rest of CLI) — research.json flagged it as the cleaner of the two. (2) It serializes concurrent writes to the SAME path within a single process — solving the in-process race when MCP tools (plan-context, rule-sections, audit-log fan-out at read-ledger.ts:98-115 which loops `for (affectedPath of affected_paths)` issuing N appends) interleave with each other AND with HTTP /api/* handlers that also append. Currently each appendFile call is independent — Node will interleave them by event-loop scheduling, not by content boundaries. (3) atomically also offers an atomic write for snapshot files (drop-in for atomicWriteText). (4) `write-file-atomic` is Node-only and lacks the in-process queue semantics. (5) Hand-rolled fs.write+fsync would replicate fsyncgate's exact failure mode (rename-on-fsync-error) — avoid. ALTERNATIVE if dep is rejected: hand-rolled in-process queue using a Map<path, Promise> chain (~25 LOC), still wrapping fs.appendFile. NOTE: atomically.appendFile uses 'a' open + write + close per call (no fsync by default) — for fsync guarantee on each event, prefer hand-rolled fs.open(path,'a')→fs.write→fs.fdatasync→fs.close (~15 LOC) plus a per-path queue (~20 LOC). For v1.7.0 stabilization, in-process queue is sufficient; full fsync is overkill (Linux ext4/macOS APFS preserve append ordering in-flight).
ledger_fix_plan.rotation_policy: DEFER for v1.7.0. Justification: events.jsonl in observed projects (ANL session, examples/werewolf-minigame-stub) is sub-100KB; growth rate is bounded by LLM call frequency (~10-100 events/session). Rotation introduces split-file consumer logic across 6 reader callsites (events.ts SSE, audit-log, read-ledger, doctor, http.ts) — each must learn to glob events.jsonl + events.jsonl.1 + events.jsonl.2.gz. Premature optimization. POST-v1.7.0 plan: (a) at append time check file size; if > 5MB, rename to events.jsonl.<ts>.archive and start fresh; (b) readers prefer events.jsonl, fall back to most recent .archive ONLY for historical queries (audit-log retentionDays); (c) gzip archives via zlib.createGzip after a 24h cool-down. EARLIER TRIGGER: if a user reports >50MB events.jsonl, ship rotation in a v1.7.x patch with a doctor warning 'event_ledger_oversized' (manual: run `fab doctor --rotate-ledger`).
ledger_fix_plan.migration_strategy:
Existing .fabric/events.jsonl files were written by raw appendFile for the entire v1.x history. Three concrete cases on first run of new code:
(1) File is well-formed (every line ends with \n, every line is valid JSON+schema): no observable behavior change. New reader returns same data, zero warnings.
(2) File has a trailing partial line (ends without \n, last segment fails parse): NEW BEHAVIOR — doctor reports event_ledger_partial_write (fixable). Without --fix, readers drop the tail (same as today via parseEventLedgerLine returning null in the legacy parseable check). With --fix, file is truncated to last \n.
(3) File has a mid-file invalid line (impossible from current writer but possible from manual edits): doctor reports event_ledger_invalid (existing manual_error code at doctor.ts:621); --fix is NOT offered for mid-file corruption (data loss risk). Recommend manual triage.
NO SCHEMA VERSION BUMP needed; events on disk already carry schema_version=1 via eventLedgerEventSchema.parse. The change is purely READER liberalization + WRITER atomicity — wire format unchanged.
RECOMMENDED first-run UX: when readEventLedger detects warnings, log a single stderr line via t() ('Detected partial write in events.jsonl (byte N); run `fab doctor --fix` to repair') gated by a process-lifetime de-dup cache (don't spam every API call).
ledger_fix_plan.atomic_write_path_changes: 1. event-ledger.ts:35 — replace `await appendFile(eventPath, line, 'utf8')` with `await ledgerWriteQueue.append(eventPath, line)` where ledgerWriteQueue is a path-keyed promise chain (in-process serializer, ~20 LOC in a new shared helper). 2. doctor.ts ensureEventLedger (L671-675) — keep as-is (single-shot empty-create at first scaffold). 3. NEW: a helper truncateLedgerToLastNewline used by the new fix code.

loc_breakdown.writer_serialization: ~25 LOC (path-keyed Promise queue helper + 1-line site change in event-ledger.ts)
loc_breakdown.reader_tail_tolerance: ~40 LOC (split-tail logic + warning surface + back-compat overload)
loc_breakdown.warning_type: ~15 LOC (LedgerWarning union + factory functions in shared)
loc_breakdown.doctor_partial_write_check: ~25 LOC (inspection branch + check creator + fix dispatch)
loc_breakdown.tests: ~55 LOC (3 new tests: trailing partial detected → fix → reads cleanly; mid-file corruption NOT auto-fixed; concurrent appends preserve line boundaries)

signal_handler_plan.current_start_file_lines: packages/server/src/index.ts:92-97 (startStdioServer body) and 131-136 (entry-guard IIFE). The StdioServerTransport.connect() resolves once the transport is wired; control returns to the IIFE which then has nothing to await — Node keeps the process alive because the stdio transport holds open file descriptors on stdin/stdout. There is NO disposer registered; SIGINT is handled by Node's default (immediate exit, skipping cleanup); SIGTERM is also default-immediate-exit; SIGHUP is ignored on most stdio configurations or kills the process.
signal_handler_plan.in_flight_tracking: missing — needs to be added. Current tools (registerPlanContext, registerRuleSections) and the bootstrap README resource handler at index.ts:74-86 do not register completion. The simplest add is to thread an InFlightTracker through createFabricServer and have tools/plan-context.ts + tools/rule-sections.ts call tracker.begin() at handler entry. Approximately 6 LOC per handler (3 handlers = 18 LOC). For HTTP server (startHttpServer L99-125) the in-flight count comes for free from server.close() callback's pending request count; no extra tracker needed. NOTE: for v1.7.0 minimum-viable, you can SKIP in-flight tracking for stdio and just do `await transport.close(); process.exit(0)` — this is safe because StdioServerTransport buffers in-flight responses and close() awaits the SDK's internal flush. But the tracker is recommended because it provides observability ('drained N requests') and a deterministic deadline.
signal_handler_plan.test_strategy:
Vitest integration test in packages/server/src/__tests__/stdio-shutdown.test.ts (~80 LOC):
1. Use child_process.spawn to launch `node dist/index.js` (or via tsx for source-mode).
2. Pipe stdin/stdout, send a JSON-RPC initialize request, await reply.
3. process.kill(child.pid, 'SIGTERM').
4. Await child exit (race with 6s timeout to bound test runtime).
5. Assert child.exitCode === 0 (clean) and child.signalCode === null.
6. Capture stderr via `child.stderr.on('data', ...)` and assert it includes 'Received SIGTERM — draining'.

A second test sends SIGTERM WHILE a long-running tool call is in flight (mock plan-context to await a controllable promise) and asserts the process waits for the call to complete OR times out at 5s. Use `vi.useRealTimers()` for the deadline portion.

A third test sends two SIGTERMs in quick succession and asserts the second triggers `process.exit(130)` immediately (cannot fully assert exit code from a child that calls process.exit(130) is signal-coded, but stderr should mention 'forcing exit').

RELATED EXISTING TEST: packages/server/src/index.test.ts asserts only the registered tool names — extend that file rather than create a new one if scope allows.

loc_breakdown.shutdown_handler: ~50 LOC (SIGINT/SIGTERM/SIGHUP install, deadline, double-signal hard exit, drain orchestration)
loc_breakdown.tests: ~80 LOC (3 vitest cases + child_process harness — but harness is reusable; net add ~40 LOC if pattern matches existing tests)

concurrency_scenarios.scenario: fabric serve (long-running MCP/HTTP) vs user runs `fabric doctor --fix` in another terminal
risk_files: .fabric/events.jsonl (serve appends per tool call; doctor --fix may invoke ensureEventLedger writeFile flag:'a' which is a no-op when file exists, OR may invoke truncateLedgerToLastNewline which RENAMES the file)
.fabric/agents.meta.json (serve calls writeRuleMeta via rule-meta-builder; doctor --fix calls writeRuleMeta — both use atomicWriteText, last-writer-wins)
.fabric/bootstrap/README.md (doctor --fix may call writeDefaultBootstrap atomicWriteText; serve does not write it but reads it via the resource handler index.ts:74-86)
concurrency_scenarios.worst_case: If doctor --fix is rewriting events.jsonl via the proposed truncateLedgerToLastNewline (atomicWriteText: write tmp + rename) WHILE serve is mid-appendFile to the original inode, the rename detaches serve's file descriptor — serve continues writing to a now-orphan inode. Result: appended events between (a) serve's open and (b) doctor's rename are LOST when the orphan inode is unlinked at process exit. Symmetric concern: if serve is reading events.jsonl via fs.open (events.ts:382) and doctor renames during the read, serve gets a stale snapshot — survivable because events.ts already handles size-shrinkage at L370-373 (offset reset).
concurrency_scenarios.mitigation: in-process queue (atomically) is INSUFFICIENT — it serializes within ONE process only. For doctor --fix vs serve we need a cross-process lock. RECOMMENDATION for v1.7.0: scope-limit doctor --fix on events.jsonl to NEVER auto-truncate when a sibling fabric serve is detected. Detection: check for `.fabric/.serve.lock` (a lockfile written by startHttpServer/startStdioServer at process start, removed on shutdown handler — ties into signal_handler_plan). If lock exists and PID is alive, doctor --fix downgrades event_ledger_partial_write to a manual_error with message 'fabric serve is running on PID N — stop it before running --fix'. NOT proper-lockfile yet — that's overkill; a simple PID file written at known path is sufficient and pairs naturally with the shutdown handlers. v1.8 can introduce proper-lockfile if the use case grows.

concurrency_scenarios.scenario: fabric serve vs user runs `fabric init --reapply` (or `fabric init --force`)
risk_files: .fabric/* (init.ts:563 rmSync(fabricDir, {force:true}) DELETES THE ENTIRE DIRECTORY when shouldReplaceWritableDirectory is true)
concurrency_scenarios.worst_case: init --reapply DESTROYS the running serve's working state. serve's open file descriptors point to deleted inodes; subsequent appends go to phantom files; SSE watchers (chokidar at events.ts:195) emit 'unlink' events that bubble to the dashboard as confused state. Worst real-world impact: lost session events. BENIGN BY DESIGN (init is documented as a project-bootstrap operation, not a runtime operation), but no guard exists.
concurrency_scenarios.mitigation: The same `.fabric/.serve.lock` PID file used above. init's preflight (init.ts:485 buildInitFabricPlan or earlier) checks for live serve lock and refuses to proceed: 'fabric serve is running on PID N. Stop it (Ctrl+C) before re-initializing.' This is a USER ERGONOMIC fix, not a locking primitive — init is interactive, the user can Ctrl+C their server.

concurrency_scenarios.scenario: Two MCP sessions of the same client (e.g. Claude Code with project + sub-project) both invoke fab_plan_context concurrently against the same projectRoot
risk_files: .fabric/events.jsonl (two writers in DIFFERENT processes both call appendEventLedgerEvent — POSIX O_APPEND atomicity holds only for writes ≤ PIPE_BUF=4096 bytes on Linux; macOS APFS atomicity is undocumented. Our event lines are typically 200-800 bytes, so de facto safe TODAY, but not guaranteed.)
concurrency_scenarios.worst_case: On a hypothetical filesystem without O_APPEND atomicity (NFS, some SMB mounts), bytes from process A interleave with bytes from process B mid-line. Read-side parseEventLedgerLine drops both lines silently. NOT observed in production but IS the case fsyncgate research warned about.
concurrency_scenarios.mitigation: BOTH proper-lockfile AND in-process queue. The in-process queue handles the 90% case (multi-tool fan-out within ONE serve process — see read-ledger.ts:98-115 which loops appendEventLedgerEvent N times per affected_path). proper-lockfile (or our PID file with flock(2) advisory lock) handles the 10% multi-process case. RECOMMENDATION: ship in-process queue in v1.7.0; defer cross-process lock to v1.8 unless we find a NFS-using customer. Document explicitly in code comment at event-ledger.ts:35: 'Append atomicity assumed at filesystem level (POSIX O_APPEND for writes < PIPE_BUF). Multi-process safety on non-local filesystems is best-effort.'

concurrency_scenarios.scenario: fabric serve receiving SIGTERM mid-appendEventLedgerEvent (e.g. SIGTERM during a fab_plan_context call that emits an event)
risk_files: .fabric/events.jsonl (the in-flight write may or may not flush to OS)
concurrency_scenarios.worst_case: If the appendFile promise has resolved (data in OS buffer) but kernel has not flushed to disk, and the OS later panics (not just process exit), the line is lost. If the appendFile promise has NOT resolved (data still in Node's libuv queue), SIGTERM-with-deadline-exit drops the data. Trailing partial line on disk is the visible artifact.
concurrency_scenarios.mitigation: Solved by signal_handler_plan + ledger_fix_plan combined: (a) signal handler awaits in-flight requests via the InFlightTracker → outstanding appends complete cleanly. (b) If deadline fires before drain, next `fabric serve` startup detects partial line (via doctor check or first read) and warns. (c) doctor --fix repairs. Three-defense-in-depth, no single point of failure.

atomic_write_inventory.blocker: none — this IS the atomic helper. Audit: the .pid.timestamp.tmp suffix is fine; consider adding fdatasync before rename for fsync guarantee (currently writeFile resolves on libuv buffer, not disk fsync).
none — agents.meta.json via atomicWriteText. Keep.
none — rule-test.index.json via atomicWriteText. Keep.
Append-only semantics REQUIRE NOT switching to atomicWriteText (would defeat the journal). Target = same appendFile but routed through path-keyed in-process queue. See ledger_fix_plan.
none — writeDefaultBootstrap via atomicWriteText. Keep, but note research finding: this writes a 70-byte STUB instead of calling buildFabricBootstrapGuide. Behavioral fix, not durability fix.

ensureEventLedger uses writeFile flag:'a' to create-empty-if-missing. With flag:'a' on an existing file, this is a no-op (no bytes written). On a missing file, creates empty. SAFE behavior. Could move to a one-line `await writeFile(path, '', { flag: 'wx' })` (exclusive create) but the difference is academic.

Init-time scaffold writes (bootstrap README). Single-shot, no concurrency, but a process crash mid-write leaves a half-written README with no .fabric/.init-context.json marker — confusing first-run state. Lift to atomicWriteText (need to import from server/services/_shared OR move helper to shared/node). Same concern as research's question on init's writeJsonAtomically (init.ts:1556).
agents.meta.json initial write. Same reasoning as L570.
INITIAL_TAXONOMY.md. Same.
events.jsonl initial empty file. writeFileSync('') is a 0-byte create — atomic by definition (no partial-write surface). Keep.
forensic.json initial write. Same as L570.
writeNewFile is one of the 7 dead-code helpers flagged by technical.json (L1383-1554). DELETE per dead-code cleanup; if kept, lift to atomic.

writeJsonAtomically: hand-rolled tmp+rename, missing fsync. Acceptable for init artifacts (codex hooks.json, claude settings.json). Could DELETE helper and call atomicWriteText (after lift to shared).
writeFileSync(guidePath, ...). bootstrap-guide is called from init scaffold AND can be called from doctor --fix (per research recommendation). Lift to atomic.

writeJsonClientConfig writes ~/.claude/claude_code_config.json or .cursor/mcp.json or similar. These are USER-FACING config files OUTSIDE .fabric/ — corrupted shape would brick the AI client's MCP config. HIGH durability priority. Lift to atomic.
Husky hook installer writes shell scripts and package.json modifications. Corruption = broken git workflow. Lift to atomic.
Codex TOML config write. Same user-facing-config concern as json.ts:59.
covered by event-ledger.ts fix
covered by event-ledger.ts fix
covered by event-ledger.ts fix
covered by event-ledger.ts fix; NOTE this is the worst-case fan-out site (per affected_path → N appends in tight loop)
covered by event-ledger.ts fix
covered by event-ledger.ts fix

atomic_write_helper_proposal.location: packages/shared/src/node/atomic-write.ts (new file in shared/node subpath, alongside detector.ts)
atomic_write_helper_proposal.rationale: Lifting atomicWriteText from server/services/_shared.ts to shared/node makes it consumable by CLI (init.ts, hooks.ts, json.ts, toml.ts, bootstrap-guide.ts). Currently CLI re-implements the pattern (writeJsonAtomically at init.ts:1556). After lift: server/services/_shared.ts re-exports from shared/node for backward compat (zero-LOC churn there); CLI imports directly. The shared/node subpath already exists for detector.ts (per shared package.json exports map).
atomic_write_helper_proposal.implementation_notes: atomicWriteText: tmp = path.<pid>.<ts>.<rand4>.tmp (add 4-char rand for crash-resume safety), writeFile(tmp), if(options.fsync) fdatasync, rename(tmp, path). atomicWriteJson: thin wrapper. createLedgerWriteQueue: returns a closure with `Map<path, Promise<void>>` chain; each append is `current = current.then(() => fs.appendFile(path, line))`; on rejection, current is reset to Promise.resolve() so subsequent calls are not poisoned. This is the in-process serializer for events.jsonl.

ledger_queue_callers: packages/server/src/services/event-ledger.ts (single import at L35; all 12 transitive append callsites benefit automatically)

questions_for_user: PID lockfile vs proper-lockfile for cross-process protection: my recommendation is a homegrown `.fabric/.serve.lock` PID file (write at startup with current PID; remove in shutdown handler; readers stat() and signal-0 to test liveness). proper-lockfile adds a runtime dep and gives true filesystem-level locking but is overkill for the use cases we have. CONFIRM: PID file approach for v1.7.0?

in-process queue: ship as a hand-rolled `createLedgerWriteQueue` (~25 LOC) in shared/node, or take the `atomically` npm dep? Recommendation: hand-rolled — fewer deps, the queue logic is trivial, atomically buys us a richer atomicWriteText too but we already have one. Confirm zero-dep route?

doctor truncateLedgerToLastNewline behavior on no-newline-at-all (single-line corruption from empty file): rename to .corrupted vs leave-and-warn. I propose rename — it preserves forensic data. Confirm?

event_ledger_truncated self-event: should the truncation operation itself emit a fabric-event recording WHAT was truncated (byte range, sample of dropped content)? Pros: full audit trail; Cons: if dropped content was schema-invalid, the truncation event may itself be hard to write. Recommendation: yes, emit a `event_ledger_truncated` event with `{byteOffset, byteLength, snippet: first120chars}`. Add the schema entry.

in-flight tracker for stdio: include in v1.7.0 or defer? Without it, signal handler does `transport.close()` and trusts the SDK's internal flush. Adding the tracker is +25 LOC in server/, +15 LOC in tools/. Recommendation: yes, ship — observability ('drained N requests' in stderr) is valuable for debugging zombie reports.
lift atomicWriteText to shared/node: yes/no? Forces CLI to depend on shared/node export (already does via detector.ts so no new direction). Confirm I should do this lift in the same PR?
init --reapply guard: refuse if `.fabric/.serve.lock` shows live PID? This is user-facing UX (interactive prompt or hard error). Recommendation: hard error with clear message. Confirm?
should we add a CI integration test that spawns `node dist/server/index.js`, sends SIGTERM, asserts clean exit? Adds ~6s to CI but locks in the fix. Recommendation: yes — high value-per-second.

_metadata.method: Read writer (event-ledger.ts), all 12 append callsites, both readers (readEventLedger + parseEventLedgerLine + the SSE tail-tolerant readEventLedgerAppendedEvents at events.ts:363-401 which ALREADY handles trailing partial in-memory but does not surface as warning), doctor inspectEventLedger (L410-444) + ensureEventLedger (L671-675). Read startStdioServer (index.ts:92-97) + entry-guard (131-136). Read init.ts scaffold writes (L561-590) + writeJsonAtomically (L1556-1560). Cross-referenced atomicWriteText (_shared.ts:12-17) and all writeFileSync/atomicWriteText callsites across packages/.
_metadata.key_insight: events.ts ALREADY has tail-tolerance for the SSE incremental reader (state.eventLedgerRemainder buffers trailing partial line at L391). The full readEventLedger path does NOT surface this — the proposal extends the same logic to the full reader and ESCALATES it from silent-drop to surfaced-warning consumed by doctor.
_metadata.non_obvious_callsite: packages/server/src/services/read-ledger.ts:98-115 is a fan-out — one ledger entry produces N appendEventLedgerEvent calls in a tight `for (affectedPath of affected_paths)` loop. This is the worst-case site for in-process interleaving with other event sources and the strongest argument for the in-process queue.
